content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 8, 2019

Keith Collins
Jones & Mayer
3777 N. Harbor Blvd
Fullerton, CA 92835

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. W-19-211

Dear Mr. Collins:

This letter responds to your request for general advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") as applicable to Mr. Alvarado's travel to Israel. Because you have not identified a specific issue regarding a particular expenditure related to this travel, we decline to provide you with written advice and instead provide you with general information. Under Regulation 18329(c)(4)(F), the Commission may decline assistance or may limit assistance to the explanation in general terms of the requirements of the Act when the facts are insufficient or too vague to render specific informal assistance.

As a general rule, payments for actual transportation costs (such as airfare, car rental), related lodging, and meals/subsistence are not subject to the gift limits if (1) paid for by a foreign government, a government agency or authority (other than the official's agency), a bona fide public or private educational institution (Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation Code), or an Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization and (2) if the travel is reasonably related to either a legislative or governmental purpose, or an issue of state, national, or international public policy. However, while not subject to gift limits, the payments must still be reported as gifts on the official's Form 700 if the payment is from a reportable source. Moreover, the payments may result in a disqualifying financial interest in decisions implicating the source of the gift.

Please review the Commission's recent Fact Sheet on the Gift of Travel for a Public Purpose, which is attached.² Because the Fact Sheet is designed to answer basic questions about this type of gift, we are no longer accepting requests for written advice on these general gift of travel issues unless the facts present a specific issue not addressed by the Fact Sheet. (See Regulation 18329(c)(4)(F), the Commission may decline assistance or limit assistance to the general

_______________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² The fact sheet is also located at http://www.fppc.ca.gov/content/dam/fppc/NS-Documents/TAD/Training-and-Outreach/077_01_2019_Travel_for_a_Public_Purpose_Paid_for_by_a_501(c)(3).pdf .

File No. W-19-211
Page No. 2

requirements of the Act when the facts are "insufficient or too vague to render specific informal
assistance." (Emphasis added.))

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Katelyn Greene
Counsel, Legal Division

KG:aja

California Fair Political Practices Commission

Gift of Travel for a Public Purpose
Paid for by a 501(c)(3) or Government Entity¹

Travel payments are NOT subject to the gift limits if:

The travel is paid for by:
• A foreign government,
• A government agency or authority (other than the official's agency),
• A bona fide public or private educational institution (Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation Code), or
• An Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization.²

AND

The official's travel is reasonably related to either:
• A legislative or governmental purpose, or
An issue of state, national, or international public policy.

For Travel payments that meet the above standard, the official:
• May accept the travel payments.
• Receives a gift that is exempt from the gift limit of $500 from a single source in a calendar year.
• Must report the gift on Form 700 if the payment is from a reportable source.
• May have a disqualifying financial interest in the source of the gift(s) (See Conflict of Interest Disqualification information below).

Exemption from the gift limit applies only to travel payments, advances, or reimbursements for actual transportation costs (such as airfare, car rental), related lodging, and meals/subsistence. (Section 89506.)

Other Costs: Payments for other items, such as a round of golf or entertainment, do not meet the travel payment exemption from the gift limit. These payments are reportable gifts, subject to the gift limit, and subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the Act. This includes payments for entertainment purposes provided during the trip, and any entertainment-related transportation or extended lodging.

¹ For other travel questions see the FPPC fact sheets, Limitations and Restrictions on Gifts, Honoraria, Travel and Loans at http://www.fppc.ca.gov/learn/public-officials-and-employees-rules-gifts-and-honoraria.html.
² This includes a person domiciled outside the United States who substantially satisfies the requirements for tax-exempt status under Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(3). Note, however, that travel paid for by nonprofits that are not Section 501(c)(3) organizations, including civic leagues, community associations, and social welfare organizations, such as chambers of commerce, does not fall under this exception.

www.fppc.ca.gov
FPPC Advice: advice@fppc.ca.gov (866.275.3772)
FPPC EAED • 077-01-2019 • Page 1 of 2

California Fair Political Practices Commission
Gift of Travel for a Public Purpose
Paid for by a 501(c)(3) or Government Entity

Conflict of Interest Disqualification: The Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using his or her position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $500 or more provided to, received by, or promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made. This prohibition includes a source of travel payments even if the payments are exempt from the $500 gift limit. (Sections 87100 and 87103(e); Regulations 18700 and 18702.4.)

Example: Mayor Manalo attends a trip to China, organized by the China Silicon Valley Business Development. The Chengdu Municipal People's Government pays for the mayor's airfare and travel costs, as well as meals and lodging on the trip. The trip's purpose is to promote job creation, and economic activity in China and the Silicon Valley. The trip agenda states that the mayor will have the opportunity to participate in meetings with local government officials, potential investors, and CEOs from local companies, and attend the Innovation Forum for Mayors of International Sister Cities of Chengdu. While in China, Mayor Manalo is also provided tickets to attend a theme-park nearby.

• Mayor Manalo must report the gift of travel on his annual Form 700 and the gift is exempt from the gift limit. The travel payments are not subject to the gift limit because the source is a foreign government and the travel is reasonably related to a governmental purpose.

• Mayor Manalo may wish to note the public purpose of the trip on his Form 700, to avoid the appearance of exceeding the gift limit.

• Mayor Manalo will have a financial interest in the source of the gift of travel, as the payments will exceed $500. He will be disqualified from participating in any government decision involving the source of the gift for twelve months following the gift.

• The theme-park tickets are not travel payments, and are not reasonably related to the public purpose of the trip. If accepted, the tickets (and payments for transportation to the park) must be reported at fair market value and are subject to the $500 gift limit.

Source of the Gift and Intermediaries: The source of the gift is the person making the payment, unless that person is acting as an intermediary. If a nonprofit organization or government entity acts as an intermediary, then the donor is the source. The gift must be reported and may be subject to the gift limits. (Regulation 18945.)

No gift is considered received or accepted if the official reimburses the donor or donor's intermediary for the gift within 30 days. (Regulation 18941(c)(3).)

Nonprofits that Regularly Host Travel for Elected Officials: A nonprofit that regularly organizes and hosts travel for elected officials and makes payments totaling more than $10,000 in a year or $5,000 for a single person for travel by an elected official must disclose certain donors. (Section 89506(f).)

www.fppc.ca.gov
FPPC Advice: advice@fppc.ca.gov (866 275 3772)
FPPC EAED • 079-01-2019 • Page 2 of 2

sections.question_synthetic: Whether payments for Mr. Alvarado's travel to Israel are subject to gift limits under the Political Reform Act's gift provisions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declines to provide specific written advice due to insufficient and vague facts, but provides general information that travel payments for transportation, lodging, and meals are not subject to gift limits if paid by qualifying sources and reasonably related to a legislative, governmental, or public policy purpose, though such payments must still be reported on Form 700 if from a reportable source and may create disqualifying financial interests.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a declination letter (File No. W-19-211). The document contains no formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections but includes general informational content about travel gift exemptions. The FPPC explicitly states it is declining to provide specific advice and instead offering general information per Regulation 18329(c)(4)(F). A fact sheet on gift of travel is attached as supplementary material.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether payments for Mr. Alvarado's travel to Israel are subject to gift limits under the Political Reform Act's gift provisions.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC declines to provide specific written advice due to insufficient and vague facts, but provides general information that travel payments for transportation, lodging, and meals are not subject to gift limits if paid by qualifying sources and reasonably related to a legislative, governmental, or public policy purpose, though such payments must still be reported on Form 700 if from a reportable source and may create disqualifying financial interests.

embedding.summary: The FPPC declines to provide specific written advice on whether Mr. Alvarado's travel to Israel constitutes a reportable gift, citing insufficient facts, but provides general guidance on travel gift exemptions and directs the requester to the Commission's Fact Sheet on Gift of Travel for Public Purposes.
